commonMistakesSection.mistake: Treating the field mapping table as a developer-only artefact without functional sign-off.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Business meaning of fields is owned by functional consultants; missing sign-off causes wrong mappings to reach production.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Make functional sign-off on the mapping table a mandatory gate before any BDC development begins.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Writing the cutover plan with estimated rather than measured timings.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Estimates from documentation are routinely wrong; cutover windows can be exceeded with no fallback.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Derive cutover step timings exclusively from mock-cutover measurements with a 20% buffer.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Documenting reconciliation as 'check in SE16N' without exact queries.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Different reviewers interpret it differently, producing inconsistent validation and missed defects.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Embed the exact ABAP/SQL reconciliation queries plus pass/fail criteria in the TS.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Skipping the rollback plan section because 'we will be careful'.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production failures happen under time pressure; without a rollback plan teams improvise badly.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Document per-object rollback approach, decision authority and error thresholds, then rehearse it in mock cutover.

bdc-data-migration-006.quickRevisionNotes:
What: A BDC migration technical specification is an 8-section blueprint covering source, target, mapping, errors, cutover, reconciliation and rollback.

Used for:
- Aligning developers, functional consultants, Basis and PM on a single source of truth
- Driving UAT test scripts and reconciliation queries
- Providing rollback and go/no-go authority during cutover

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- Field mapping table format: Source | Transformation | SAP Field | Mandatory | Default
- SHDB recording section with dynpro numbers and FNAM list
- Cutover plan with measured timings from mock runs

Interview keyword: 'Eight-section TS with signed mapping table and rollback rehearsed in mock cutover.'

followupAnswerBank.question: Why is field mapping table the most important part of BDC technical specification?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Field mapping table developer aur functional consultant ke beech ka contract hai — yeh exactly define karta hai ki kaunsa source field kaunse SAP field mein map hota hai kis transformation ke saath. Iske bina developers assumptions banate hain jo production mein incorrect data cause karte hain. Table mein include hona chahiye: source field name aur data type, transformation rule (date format, leading zero padding, code mapping), target SAP table aur field, mandatory hai ya nahi, aur source blank hone pe default value. Yeh document development start hone se pehle functional sign-off require karta hai — post-development mapping changes expensive aur error-prone hote hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The field mapping table is the contract between developer and functional consultant — it defines exactly which source field maps to which SAP field with what transformation. Without it, developers make assumptions that cause incorrect data in production. The table must include: source field name and data type, transformation rule (date format, leading zero padding, code mapping), target SAP table and field, whether mandatory, and default value if source is blank. This document requires functional sign-off before development starts — post-development mapping changes are expensive and error-prone.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project lesson: developer mapped source 'Customer Type' code 'C' to SAP account group 'KUNA' — functional team meant 'DEBI'. Mapping table had been verbal, not documented. Wrong account group — customers created with wrong master data. 2000 records had to be corrected. If mapping table had been signed off, this would have been caught in review.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Creating field mapping table only for developer reference without functional review and sign-off — the functional consultant knows the business meaning of each field, the developer knows the SAP target. Both perspectives are needed.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Field mapping table documented as comments in code
" Source: LEGACY_CUSTOMER table | Target: KNA1
" SRC: CUST_CODE (VARCHAR 10) → KNA1-KUNNR (CHAR 10)
"   Rule: Pad with leading zeros to 10 chars
"   lv_kunnr = |{ ls_src-cust_code ALPHA = IN WIDTH = 10 }|
" SRC: CUST_TYPE (CHAR 1: C/I/G) → KNA1-KTOKD (CHAR 4)
"   Rule: C→DEBI, I→KUNA, G→KORG  (agreed with functional)
"   lv_ktokd = SWITCH #( ls_src-cust_type
"              WHEN 'C' THEN 'DEBI' WHEN 'I' THEN 'KUNA'
"              WHEN 'G' THEN 'KORG' ELSE 'DEBI' ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you document the cutover plan in a BDC migration technical spec?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: TS ke cutover plan section mein include hota hai: source system ke liye freeze date aur time (is point ke baad no new changes), migration sequence har object type ke liye estimated start aur end times ke saath, error rate thresholds aur decision authority ke saath go/no-go decision checkpoints, functional team sign-off ke liye validation window timeline, planned go-live time, escalation path ke saath contact list, aur rollback trigger criteria. Timings mock cutover runs se aate hain — kabhi bina kam se kam ek dress rehearsal run kiye estimate mat karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The cutover plan section of the TS includes: freeze date and time for the source system (no new changes after this point), migration sequence with estimated start and end times for each object type, go/no-go decision checkpoints with error rate thresholds and decision authority, validation window timeline for functional team sign-off, planned go-live time, contact list with escalation path, and rollback trigger criteria. Timings come from mock cutover runs — never estimate without running at least one dress rehearsal.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Our TS cutover plan had timestamps per job: Vendor master 10PM-1AM (3h), Customer master 1AM-4AM (3h), Material master 4AM-7AM (3h), Open items 7AM-10AM (3h), Validation 10AM-12PM (2h), Go-live 12PM. Total: 14 hours window. Mock cutover confirmed 12 hours actual. 2 hour buffer used for an unexpected open item reconciliation issue.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Writing cutover plan without timing from actual mock run — estimated timings from documentation are always wrong. Always derive cutover timings from measured mock cutover runs.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Cutover sequence documentation (code comment format)
" CUTOVER PLAN — Project: Vendor Migration — Date: 2024-11-29
" Step 1: 22:00 — ZMIG_VENDOR_MASTER (5000 records, est 2h)
"   Error threshold: >3% → escalate. >10% → rollback decision.
" Step 2: 00:00 — ZMIG_VENDOR_BANK (5000 records, est 1h)
"   Prerequisite: Step 1 complete with <3% error
" Step 3: 01:00 — ZMIG_OPEN_ITEMS (20000 records, est 3h)
" Checkpoint: 04:00 — Go/No-Go review by PM + Functional Lead
" Step 4: 09:00 — Validation run by functional team
" Go-live: 11:00 or after functional sign-off

followupAnswerBank.question: What is a reconciliation query and how do you document it in the TS?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Reconciliation query section specific, executable ABAP SELECT statements ya reports document karta hai jo migration completeness validate karte hain. Main document karta hoon: count reconciliation query source file record count ko SAP records created ke against compare karta hai, monetary objects ke liye financial total reconciliation source ke sum ko SAP ke against compare karta hai, spot-check query 20 random migrated records ka list manual field verification ke liye, aur koi bhi object-specific business validations. Section specify karta hai ki kaun har query run karta hai, cutover mein kis point pe, aur kya pass/fail constitute karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The reconciliation query section documents specific, executable ABAP SELECT statements or reports that validate migration completeness. I document: count reconciliation query comparing source file record count against SAP records created, financial total reconciliation for monetary objects comparing sum from source against SAP, spot-check query listing 20 random migrated records for manual field verification, and any object-specific business validations. The section specifies who runs each query, at what point in the cutover, and what constitutes pass/fail.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Vendor open items TS reconciliation section: Query 1: COUNT of BSIK records per vendor where posting date in migration range. Query 2: SUM(DMBTR) per vendor. Expected: count matches source file count exactly, amounts match to last decimal. Variance = escalation to project manager. This section was directly used by the functional team during cutover validation — they ran it themselves without developer involvement.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Documenting reconciliation as 'verify in SE16N' without specifying exact query — different people interpret this differently, leading to inconsistent validation. Always provide the exact executable query.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Reconciliation queries documented in TS
" Query 1: Count check
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM kna1
  WHERE kunnr IN ( SELECT kunnr FROM ztmig_loaded
                   WHERE run_id = 'PROD_2024_01' )
  INTO @DATA(lv_sap_cnt).
" Compare with: source file count = 5000
" Pass criterion: lv_sap_cnt = 5000
" Query 2: Financial total (for FI objects)
SELECT SUM( dmbtr ) FROM bsik
  WHERE lifnr IN @lt_migrated_vendors
  INTO @DATA(lv_total).
" Compare with: source system total = 1234567.89
" Pass criterion: ABS( lv_total - 1234567.89 ) = 0

followupAnswerBank.question: How should you document error handling design in a BDC technical specification?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Error handling design documentation cover karta hai: har CALL TRANSACTION ke baad exact sy-subrc check, error log table structure key fields (record key, original input values, SAP message type aur text, timestamp, username) ke saath, functional team ko present hone wali error report ka format, correction workflow (functional team source data fix karti hai, corrected file provide karti hai, developer sirf failed records ke liye re-run karta hai), duplicates prevent karne ke liye re-run safety mechanism, aur un records ke liye escalation path jo multiple correction attempts ke baad bhi repeatedly fail hote hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Error handling design documentation covers: the exact sy-subrc check after each CALL TRANSACTION, the error log table structure with key fields (record key, original input values, SAP message type and text, timestamp, username), the format of the error report presented to the functional team, the correction workflow (functional team fixes source data, provides corrected file, developer re-runs for failed records only), re-run safety mechanism to prevent duplicates, and escalation path for records that fail repeatedly after multiple correction attempts.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Error handling section of our TS included a process flow: Failed record → Error log table ZTMIG_ERRORS → ALV report → Email to functional lead → Functional team corrects Excel → Developer loads corrected file → Re-run with mode 'RERUN' that skips already-processed records. This process was followed exactly during cutover — functional team knew exactly what to do when they got the error report.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Documenting error handling only as 'errors will be logged' without specifying the log structure, report format, correction process, and re-run capability — this leaves functional team without a clear action path when they receive a list of failed records.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Error handling: TS-defined structure
TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_error_log,
  run_id   TYPE string,          " Migration run identifier
  rec_key  TYPE string,          " Primary key (e.g. customer number)
  input_01 TYPE string,          " Original field 1 value
  input_02 TYPE string,          " Original field 2 value
  msg_type TYPE c LENGTH 1,      " E/W/I from SAP
  msg_text TYPE string,          " SAP error message text
  uname    TYPE sy-uname,
  ertime   TYPE timestampl,
END OF ty_error_log.
" This structure approved by functional team in TS review

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the rollback plan section in a migration TS and what must it contain?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Rollback plan section mein contain hona chahiye: reversal ke liye use kiye gaye specific SAP transaction ya custom program ke saath per-object reversal approach, har object type ke liye rollback complete karne ka estimated time, error rate threshold jo rollback decision trigger karta hai, decision authority (typically project manager client sign-off ke saath), execution steps aur responsible persons, aur rollback trigger hone pe stakeholders ko inform karne ka communication plan. Critical: kuch objects jaise FI posted documents easily reverse nahi ho sakte — un ke liye, strategy delayed commit hai (finalize karne se pehle validate karo) ya management sign-off ke saath manual reversal cost accept karna.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The rollback plan section must contain: per-object reversal approach with the specific SAP transaction or custom program used for reversal, estimated time to complete rollback for each object type, the error rate threshold that triggers rollback decision, the decision authority (typically project manager with client sign-off), execution steps and responsible persons, and a communication plan for informing stakeholders if rollback is triggered. Critical: some objects like FI posted documents cannot be easily reversed — for these, the strategy is delayed commit (validate before finalizing) or accepting manual reversal cost with management sign-off.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Our TS rollback plan: vendor master — ZMIG_ROLLBACK_VENDOR program (custom mass deletion, 30 min for 5000 records), customer master — standard mass deletion via ZSAPDELCUST, open items — session not committed until validation complete (30-second rollback). Trigger: PM + client decides at go/no-go checkpoint if >5% error. This plan was rehearsed in mock cutover.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Writing 'rollback by deleting records' without having a tested deletion program ready — manual deletion of 5000 records takes days, not hours. Build and test the rollback program as part of migration development.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Rollback program skeleton (developed alongside migration)
REPORT zmig_rollback_vendor.
SELECT lifnr FROM ztmig_loaded
  WHERE run_id = p_runid AND status = 'S'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_to_delete).
" Delete only records this run created
" No standard BAPI for vendor delete — use XK06 or archiving (SARA)
LOOP AT lt_to_delete ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<v>).
  " Call XK06 via BDC, or trigger archiving — no direct BAPI exists
ENDLOOP.
CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT' EXPORTING wait = 'X'.
WRITE: / |Rollback complete: { lines( lt_to_delete ) } records reversed|.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does the TS documentation differ between BDC and BAPI migration approaches?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BDC technical spec mein SHDB recording documentation include hota hai dynpro numbers aur screen sequence ke saath, BDC FNAM field name table, MODE parameter strategy, aur popup handling notes — sab screen simulation ke specific. BAPI technical spec inhe replace karta hai: specific BAPI function module name with its parameter structure (import/export/tables), commit strategy (BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT ke liye batch size), aur BAPI return code handling logic. Dono common sections share karte hain: source file layout, field mapping table, error handling, cutover plan, reconciliation, aur rollback. BAPI spec typically shorter aur zyada maintainable hota hai — SAP patches ke baad update karne ke liye koi screen-specific documentation nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDC technical spec includes SHDB recording documentation with dynpro numbers and screen sequence, BDC FNAM field name table, MODE parameter strategy, and popup handling notes — all specific to screen simulation. BAPI technical spec replaces these with: the specific BAPI function module name with its parameter structure (import/export/tables), commit strategy (batch size for BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT), and BAPI return code handling logic. Both share common sections: source file layout, field mapping table, error handling, cutover plan, reconciliation, and rollback. The BAPI spec is typically shorter and more maintainable — no screen-specific documentation to update after SAP patches.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: We maintained two types of TS templates in our team: BDC_TS_TEMPLATE.docx and BAPI_TS_TEMPLATE.docx. Developer chose correct template at project start. Client review was faster for BAPI template — no screen documentation to review. BDC template review took 2 hours, BAPI template 45 minutes. Functional team found BAPI mapping table easier to understand.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using the same TS template for both BDC and BAPI without including or excluding relevant sections — BAPI TS with SHDB documentation is confusing, BDC TS without screen sequence is incomplete.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" BAPI TS documentation format (in code comments)
" BAPI: BAPI_CUSTOMER_CREATEFROMDATA1
" IMPORT params (verify in SE37):
"   pi_personaldata: personal/address data (name, street, city)
"   pi_companydata:  company-code-level data (recon acct, terms)
" TABLES params:
"   return: result messages (type E = error)
" Commit: BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT every 500 records
" Error check: IF line_exists( return[ type = 'E' ] )
" No dynpro documentation needed — BAPI is screen-independent

followupAnswerBank.question: Who should review and sign off on a BDC migration technical specification?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BDC migration TS ke liye sign-off honi chahiye: functional consultant se field mapping business accuracy aur error handling usability confirm karte hue, technical lead se code approach aur performance strategy validate karte hue, Basis team se cutover window approve karte hue aur authorization setup feasibility confirm karte hue, aur ideally client project manager se cutover plan aur go/no-go thresholds acknowledge karte hue. Har sign-off ka specific scope hai — functional business logic validate karta hai, technical technical approach validate karta hai, Basis infrastructure validate karta hai, client timeline validate karta hai. Kisi bhi ek ka missing gap create karta hai jo production issue ke roop mein surface hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDC migration TS should have sign-off from: functional consultant confirming field mapping business accuracy and error handling usability, technical lead validating code approach and performance strategy, Basis team approving cutover window and confirming authorization setup feasibility, and ideally client project manager acknowledging the cutover plan and go/no-go thresholds. Each sign-off has a specific scope — functional validates business logic, technical validates technical approach, Basis validates infrastructure, client validates timeline. Missing any one creates a gap that surfaces as a production issue.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Lesson from project: TS had functional and technical sign-off but not Basis. Cutover day: Basis team said background jobs cannot run on Sunday due to maintenance window. Cutover had to be rescheduled by 2 weeks. If Basis had reviewed TS, they would have flagged this conflict in week 2 of the project, not week 8.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Getting only developer and functional sign-off on TS while skipping Basis and client — infrastructure constraints and business calendar conflicts discovered in TS review are far cheaper to resolve than cutover weekend surprises.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS document header (approval tracking)
" Document: BDC Technical Specification — Customer Master Migration
" Version: 1.2  Status: Approved
" Prepared by: Developer Name  Date: 2024-10-01
" Reviewed by:
"   Functional Lead: Jane Doe  Sign-off: 2024-10-05
"   Technical Lead:  John Smith Sign-off: 2024-10-06
"   Basis Lead:      Bob Kumar  Sign-off: 2024-10-07
"   Project Manager: Sara Lee   Sign-off: 2024-10-08
" Change log: v1.1 field mapping updated, v1.2 cutover window revised

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you document the source file layout in a BDC technical specification?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Source file layout documentation include karta hai: file format aur encoding, delimiter specification, header row presence, ek numbered column table column name, data type, maximum field length, mandatory/optional flag, dates aur numbers ke format rules, code fields ke valid value list, aur ek sample row ke saath. Yeh document functional team ko data preparation template ke roop mein provide hota hai — woh exactly is specification ke baad data fill karte hain. Specification se deviations pre-migration validation program mein catch hote hain aur kisi bhi BDC run se pehle report ho jaate hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Source file layout documentation includes: file format and encoding, delimiter specification, header row presence, a numbered column table with column name, data type, maximum field length, mandatory/optional flag, format rules for dates and numbers, valid value list for code fields, and a sample row. This document is provided to the functional team as the data preparation template — they fill data following exactly this specification. Deviations from the specification are caught in the pre-migration validation program and reported before any BDC run.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Source file specification sent to functional team. First file received: dates in MM-DD-YYYY (hyphens not slashes), amounts with currency symbols (USD 1,234.56), some mandatory columns blank. Validation program caught all 3 issues before BDC ran. Functional team corrected and resubmitted. Second file: 0 validation errors. BDC ran cleanly. The specification document prevented bad data from reaching BDC.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Sending a vague 'please prepare the data in Excel' instruction to functional team without a detailed column specification — results in inconsistent data formats that the validation program and BDC program must handle ad-hoc.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Source file specification validation
" Validate date column format BEFORE BDC run
CONSTANTS: c_date_pattern TYPE string VALUE '##/##/####'.
LOOP AT lt_source ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<r>).
  " Check date column format MM/DD/YYYY
  IF NOT <r>-open_date CO '0123456789/'.
    APPEND VALUE #( col = 'OPEN_DATE'
                    val = <r>-open_date
                    err = 'Invalid date format — use MM/DD/YYYY' ) TO lt_val_errors.
  ENDIF.
  " Check mandatory field
  IF <r>-cust_code IS INITIAL.
    APPEND VALUE #( col = 'CUST_CODE' err = 'Mandatory field blank' ) TO lt_val_errors.
  ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.

bdc-data-migration-007.easyUnderstanding: Effort estimation matlab pehle estimate karo kitna kaam hai, phir project mein accordingly plan karo. BDC estimation ke simple benchmarks jo real projects se aaye hain: Simple BDC (5-10 screens, ek transaction) — 3 se 5 din. Complex BDC (multiple transactions, error handling, parallel processing) — 2 se 3 hafte. LSMW (configuration-based, no coding) — 1 se 2 din per object. Ek pura module migration (jaise sab vendors) — 4 se 6 hafte including testing. Yeh benchmarks tab ke liye hain jab developer experienced hai aur requirements clear hain. Agar requirements unclear hain toh 20-30% buffer add karo. Agar naya developer hai toh 50% add karo. Estimation mein sirf coding time mat socho — design, unit test, UAT support, error fix, re-run, documentation bhi hote hain. Real estimation formula: (development + unit test) * 1.5 for buffer + UAT support time + documentation time.
bdc-data-migration-007.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Effort estimation ek skill hai jo junior aur senior developer ko alag karta hai. Interview mein 'how do you estimate BDC effort' poochha jaaye toh structured answer do:

Pehla step — Requirements analysis: Estimate start karne se pehle requirements clear karo. Kitne source fields hain? Kitni transactions hain? Kya multiple screens chahiye? Error handling kitna complex hoga? Volume kitna hai? Yeh questions answer kiye bina estimate impossible hai.

Doosra step — Complexity classification:
- Simple BDC: Single transaction, 5-10 screens, straightforward field mapping, 500-5000 records. Estimate: 3-5 days (design 0.5 day, SHDB + code 2 days, unit test 1 day, documentation 0.5 day).
- Medium BDC: Multiple transactions ya screens (10-20), complex field transformations (date formats, code mapping, lookups), error handling with ALV report, 5000-50000 records. Estimate: 8-12 days.
- Complex BDC: Multiple dependent transactions (create vendor, then bank details, then contact person — sequence dependent), large volume (100K+) requiring parallel processing, integration with other modules, custom rollback program. Estimate: 15-20 days.
- LSMW: Standard object, configuration-based, no ABAP. Estimate: 1-2 days per object.
- Full module migration: All objects in one SAP module (all vendors including master + bank + open items). Estimate: 4-6 weeks including 2 weeks for testing.

Teesra step — Non-coding effort: Developers underestimate non-coding work. Add karo: Requirements clarification (10-15% of dev estimate), Unit testing (30-40% of dev estimate), UAT support — functional team queries answer karna, error investigation (20-30% of dev estimate), Documentation (10-15%), Knowledge transfer (5-10%), Buffer for scope creep (20%).

Chauta step — Volume-based adjustment: 100K+ records mein parallel processing design karna padta hai — add 2-3 days for architecture. Network/system latency for large volume test runs — add 1-2 days for performance testing and optimization.

Paanchva step — Risk factors: Unclear mapping — add 20%. New developer — add 50%. Legacy system without documentation — add 30%. Complex validations — add 25%.
bdc-data-migration-007.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'I estimate BDC and data migration effort using complexity classification. For a simple BDC — single transaction, 5-10 screens, standard field mapping — I estimate 3 to 5 days covering design, SHDB recording, development, unit test with error cases, and documentation. For complex multi-transaction BDC with large volume requiring parallel processing, the estimate is 2 to 3 weeks. LSMW configuration for a standard object is 1 to 2 days. A full module migration covering all objects with testing is 4 to 6 weeks. I always add 20% buffer for scope clarifications and apply a multiplier for unclear requirements. Critically, my estimate includes not just coding time but also UAT support, error investigation, re-run support, and documentation — non-coding work is typically 40 to 50% of total effort in my experience.'

Hinglish tip: Interviewer impression ke liye yeh add karo: 'Mere estimate mein non-coding time bhi hota hai — requirements clarification, UAT support, error investigation. Yeh 40-50% hota hai total effort ka. Jo developers sirf coding time estimate karte hain unke projects always delay hote hain.' Yeh mature, experience-backed statement hai jo interviewer ko impress karta hai.
bdc-data-migration-007.codeExamples:
" Effort estimation support: automated complexity indicator
REPORT zest_bdc_complexity.
PARAMETERS: p_screens TYPE i DEFAULT 5,
             p_trans   TYPE i DEFAULT 1,
             p_volume  TYPE i DEFAULT 1000,
             p_errors  TYPE c AS CHECKBOX DEFAULT 'X'.
DATA: lv_days TYPE p DECIMALS 1.
" Base estimate by screen count
lv_days = SWITCH #( p_screens
  WHEN 1 THEN 1  WHEN 2 THEN 2  ELSE p_screens / 3 ).
" Adjust for multiple transactions
lv_days = lv_days + ( p_trans - 1 ) * 2.
" Adjust for volume (>10K needs performance work)
IF p_volume > 10000. lv_days = lv_days + 2. ENDIF.
" Error handling + testing overhead
IF p_errors = 'X'.  lv_days = lv_days * 1.4. ENDIF.
" 20% buffer
lv_days = lv_days * 1.2.
WRITE: / |Estimated effort: { lv_days } days (includes test + doc)|.
bdc-data-migration-007.interviewScriptHinglish: BDC effort estimate main complexity tiers use karke karta hoon — simple BDC 3-5 days, medium 8-12 days, complex 15-20 days, LSMW 1-2 days per object. Estimate mein hamesha non-coding effort bhi include karta hoon — requirements clarification, unit testing, UAT support, mock cutover aur documentation — jo typically coding time pe 40-50% extra add karta hai. Main kabhi bhi estimate finalize nahi karta bina real sample file review kiye, kyunki clients ka 'simple' often format variety aur complex mappings chhupata hai. Business value yeh hai ki realistic timelines milti hain, scope-change communication transparent hoti hai aur cutover delivery predictable hota hai.
bdc-data-migration-007.interviewScriptEnglish: I estimate BDC effort using complexity tiers: simple BDC 3-5 days, medium 8-12 days, complex 15-20 days, LSMW 1-2 days per object. The estimate always includes non-coding effort — requirements clarification, unit testing, UAT support, mock cutover and documentation — which typically adds 40-50% on top of coding time. I never finalise an estimate without reviewing a real sample file, since 'simple' from clients often hides format variety and complex mappings. The business value is realistic timelines, transparent scope-change communication and predictable cutover delivery.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Estimating in the first meeting without seeing the actual source file.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Real data uncovers date format variety, code mismatches and unexpected mandatory fields hidden behind the word 'simple'.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Always request a 20-50 row sample, analyse it for 30 minutes, then commit to an estimate.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Adding only a 10-20% testing buffer to BDC estimates.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Testing in migration is 80-100% of development effort, not 20%; small buffers cause systematic overruns.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Use a testing factor of 1.5-2x of development, including unit, UAT support, bug-fix and mock cutover.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Documenting the volume assumption only in chat instead of the estimate.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: When volume doubles silently, developers absorb the work, hiding overrun until it is too late to react.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: State the exact record-count assumption in the estimate and trigger a re-estimate on any deviation above 20%.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Absorbing additional scope without raising a change request.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Project manager and client lose visibility, leading to broken commitments and developer burnout.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Formally email the revised effort breakdown and timeline impact whenever scope expands.

bdc-data-migration-007.quickRevisionNotes:
What: BDC effort estimation classifies work by complexity, volume and risk and adds explicit non-coding effort and buffers.

Used for:
- Setting realistic project timelines for migrations
- Choosing BDC vs LSMW vs BAPI based on effort impact
- Communicating scope-change impact transparently

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- Complexity tiers: simple 3-5d, medium 8-12d, complex 15-20d, LSMW 1-2d/object
- Non-coding adders: requirements 10%, testing 30-40%, UAT 20-25%, docs 10%, mock cutover 1d/run
- Risk multipliers: unclear reqs +20%, new dev +50%, unknown data +25%

Interview keyword: 'Tiered estimation with non-coding effort and explicit volume assumption.'

followupAnswerBank.question: What are the key inputs you need before estimating BDC migration effort?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Estimate karne se pehle mujhe chahiye: source system type aur file format, target SAP transaction screens ki number ke saath, record volume, field mapping complexity including any transformations ya code lookups, error handling requirements, rollback program needed hai ya nahi, cutover timeline constraints, aur team experience level. Inke bina koi bhi estimate ek guess hai. Main estimates finalize karne se pehle sample source file aur 10-record test dataset maangta hoon — yeh hidden complexities reveal karte hain jaise special characters, code mismatches, aur unexpected mandatory fields.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Before estimating I need: source system type and file format, target SAP transaction with number of screens, record volume, field mapping complexity including any transformations or code lookups, error handling requirements, whether a rollback program is needed, cutover timeline constraints, and team experience level. Without these inputs any estimate is a guess. I ask for a sample source file and a 10-record test dataset before finalizing estimates — these reveal hidden complexities like special characters, code mismatches, and unexpected mandatory fields.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Estimation meeting: client said 'simple customer migration, about 1000 records.' I asked for sample file. Sample file had 80 columns, 15 different date formats, 6 legacy system codes that mapped to SAP in non-obvious ways, and records in 3 languages. Revised estimate: 8 days (not the 3 I would have said without the sample file).
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Providing estimates in the first meeting before seeing actual data — the phrase 'simple migration' from a client almost always hides complexity that only the data reveals.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Complexity assessment from sample data
LOOP AT lt_sample ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<r>).
  " Count distinct date formats
  IF <r>-date_col CA '-'. lv_dash_dates = lv_dash_dates + 1. ENDIF.
  IF <r>-date_col CA '/'. lv_slash_dates = lv_slash_dates + 1. ENDIF.
  " Count distinct code values needing mapping
  COLLECT VALUE #( code = <r>-type_code ) INTO lt_distinct_codes.
ENDLOOP.
WRITE: / |Date format variants: { lv_dash_dates } dash, { lv_slash_dates } slash|.
WRITE: / |Distinct type codes to map: { lines( lt_distinct_codes ) }|.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate testing effort for BDC migration?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BDC migration ke liye testing effort: unit testing development time ka 30-40% hai aur yeh developer karta hai — positive cases, error cases, re-run safety, aur MODE N behavior test karna. UAT support jahan functional team validate karti hai development ka 20-25% hai — developer questions, error investigation, aur quick fixes ke liye available rehta hai. UAT ke baad bug fixing 15-20% add karta hai. Large volume migrations ke liye dedicated performance testing phase 1-2 days ka add karo. Mock cutover support 1 day per mock run hai minimum 2 mocks recommended ke saath. Total testing effort typically total development effort ke equal hota hai — 5-day development 10-day total project hai including all testing phases.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Testing effort for BDC migration: unit testing is 30-40% of development time and is done by the developer — testing positive cases, error cases, re-run safety, and MODE N behavior. UAT support where the functional team validates is 20-25% of development — developer is available for questions, error investigation, and quick fixes. Bug fixing after UAT rounds adds 15-20%. For large volume migrations add a dedicated performance testing phase of 1-2 days. Mock cutover support is 1 day per mock run with minimum 2 mocks recommended. Total testing effort typically equals total development effort — a 5-day development is a 10-day total project including all testing phases.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Estimation retrospective: development took 4 days as estimated. Testing breakdown: unit test 1.5 days, UAT support 3 days (functional team found 2 mapping bugs + 1 error handling issue), bug fix 1 day, mock cutover support 2 days. Total: 7.5 days testing for 4 days development. Ratio: 1.9x. Now I always estimate total project as 2x development estimate.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Estimating only development time and adding '20% for testing' — testing in data migration is closer to 80-100% of development effort, not 20%. Underestimating testing causes project delays.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Effort breakdown template (used in project planning)
" BDC Migration: Customer Master (Simple — 8 screens, 5000 records)
" Development phases:
"   Requirements + SHDB analysis:  0.5 days
"   BDC program development:       2.0 days
"   Error handling + log:          0.5 days
"   Documentation:                 0.5 days
"   Subtotal development:          3.5 days
" Testing phases:
"   Unit test (positive + error):  1.5 days
"   UAT support:                   1.5 days
"   Bug fix post-UAT:              0.5 days
"   Mock cutover support:          1.0 day
"   Subtotal testing:              4.5 days
" TOTAL ESTIMATE:                  8.0 days

followupAnswerBank.question: How does migration volume affect effort estimation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Volume runtime aur architectural complexity dono affect karta hai. 5000 records ke neeche: standard sequential BDC ya BAPI sufficient hai, no special architecture. 5000 se 50000 tak: package-based processing aur performance test phase add karo — 1 additional day. 50000 se 500000: parallel background job design, checkpoint restart mechanism, performance tuning — 3 se 5 additional days. 500000 se upar: full parallel architecture, possibly database partitioning considerations, system resource allocation ke liye Basis team coordination — 5 se 7 additional days. Estimate mein volume assumption document karo — agar volume significantly change ho toh estimate revise karna padega.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Volume affects both runtime and architectural complexity. Below 5000 records: standard sequential BDC or BAPI suffices, no special architecture. 5000 to 50000: add package-based processing and a performance test phase — 1 additional day. 50000 to 500000: parallel background job design, checkpoint restart mechanism, performance tuning — 3 to 5 additional days. Above 500000: full parallel architecture, possibly database partitioning considerations, Basis team coordination for system resource allocation — 5 to 7 additional days. Document the volume assumption in the estimate — if volume changes significantly, the estimate must be revised.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Initial estimate for material master: 8000 records — 5 days. Halfway through project client revealed actual volume: 85,000 records. Parallel processing architecture needed: 3 additional days design. Performance test: 1 additional day. Restart mechanism: 1 day. Total addition: 5 days — estimate became 10 days. Volume assumption change = estimate change. Always document volume assumption explicitly.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not documenting the volume assumption in the estimate — when volume doubles, developers absorb the extra work without scope change discussion, causing untracked overrun.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Volume-based architecture decision
CONSTANTS: c_simple_limit   TYPE i VALUE 5000,
           c_parallel_limit TYPE i VALUE 50000.
IF lv_total_records <= c_simple_limit.
  " Simple sequential: no special architecture
  PERFORM run_sequential USING lt_all_records.
ELSEIF lv_total_records <= c_parallel_limit.
  " Package processing: loop with package size
  PERFORM run_packaged USING lt_all_records c_pkg_size.
ELSE.
  " Parallel jobs: SM36 scheduling + restart logic needed
  " This path requires 3-5 extra dev days — flag in estimate
  PERFORM run_parallel USING p_from p_to.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between development effort and project effort in migration estimation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Development effort sirf coding activities cover karta hai: SHDB recording, program development, aur developer-run unit testing. Project effort requirements gathering se go-live tak ki saari activities include karta hai: requirements clarification (10-15%), technical specification writing (10%), development (35-40%), error cases including unit testing (15%), UAT support aur bug fixing (20-25%), documentation (5-10%), aur cutover support including mock runs (10-15%). Project effort typically development effort se 2.5 se 3x hota hai. Sirf development time estimate karke aur use project timeline ke roop mein present karke consistent project overruns hote hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Development effort covers only coding activities: SHDB recording, program development, and developer-run unit testing. Project effort includes all activities from requirements gathering to go-live: requirements clarification (10-15%), technical specification writing (10%), development (35-40%), unit testing including error cases (15%), UAT support and bug fixing (20-25%), documentation (5-10%), and cutover support including mock runs (10-15%). Project effort is typically 2.5 to 3x development effort. Estimating only development time and presenting it as project timeline causes consistent project overruns.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Estimation to stakeholders: 'BDC will take 5 days to develop.' Stakeholder plans 5-day timeline. Actual project: requirements 2 days, development 5 days, testing 4 days, UAT 3 days, bug fix 2 days, documentation 1 day, mock cutover 2 days. Total: 19 days. Stakeholder upset. Now I always present project effort not just development effort.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Presenting development-only estimates as project timelines to stakeholders — leads to unrealistic expectations and project delivery conflicts. Always break down full project effort when presenting to project managers.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Estimation breakdown for stakeholder presentation
" Phase 1 — Analysis & Design
"   Source file analysis + requirements:  2 days
"   Technical specification writing:       1 day
"   TS review + sign-off:                 1 day  (depends on availability)
" Phase 2 — Development
"   SHDB recording + BDC coding:          3 days
"   Error handling + validation:          1 day
"   Unit testing (all cases):             1.5 days
" Phase 3 — Testing & Validation
"   UAT support + bug fixing:             3 days
"   Performance testing (if >10K recs):   1 day
" Phase 4 — Cutover
"   Mock cutover x2 runs:                 2 days
"   Actual cutover support:               1 day
"   Documentation + KT:                  1 day
" TOTAL PROJECT EFFORT: ~17.5 days

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort for LSMW versus custom BDC development?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: LSMW estimation typically per standard migration object 1 se 2 days hota hai — 0.5 day setup aur object selection ke liye, 0.5 se 1 day field mapping configuration aur testing ke liye, 0.5 day production run aur validation ke liye. No ABAP coding ka matlab no compilation, no ABAP unit test. Same object ke liye custom BDC se compare karo: 3 se 5 days. 10 standard objects wale projects ke liye, LSMW custom BDC ke versus 20 se 30 development days save karta hai. Tradeoff: LSMW less flexible hai aur SAP-delivered objects tak limited. Estimation decision: saare migration objects enumerate karo, har ek ko LSMW-suitable ya custom BDC requiring classify karo, respective estimates sum karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: LSMW estimation is typically 1 to 2 days per standard migration object — 0.5 day for setup and object selection, 0.5 to 1 day for field mapping configuration and testing, 0.5 day for production run and validation. No ABAP coding means no compilation, no ABAP unit test. Compare this to custom BDC for the same object: 3 to 5 days. For projects with 10 standard objects, LSMW saves 20 to 30 development days versus custom BDC. The tradeoff: LSMW is less flexible and limited to SAP-delivered objects. Estimation decision: enumerate all migration objects, classify each as LSMW-suitable or requiring custom BDC, sum respective estimates.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Migration project with 20 objects: 12 standard (customer, vendor, material, GL accounts, cost centers) — LSMW suitable: 12 * 1.5 days = 18 days. 8 custom Z-transactions — BDC required: 8 * 4 days = 32 days. Total: 50 days. All custom BDC: 20 * 4 = 80 days. LSMW saved 30 days = significant project budget saving. This analysis should be done at project planning stage.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Defaulting to custom BDC for all objects without checking if LSMW can handle standard objects — losing 2-3 days per object that could have been done in LSMW.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" LSMW vs BDC decision per migration object
" Object 1: Customer master — LSMW standard object exists
"   Method: LSMW (RMDATIND customer)  Estimate: 1.5 days
" Object 2: Material master — LSMW standard object exists
"   Method: LSMW (RMDATIND material)  Estimate: 1.5 days
" Object 3: Custom price table ZTPRICE — no LSMW object
"   Method: Custom BDC for ZPRC01     Estimate: 4 days
" Object 4: Vendor master — LSMW standard object exists
"   Method: LSMW                       Estimate: 1 day
" Total: 8 days (vs 16 days all BDC) — LSMW saves 8 days

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you communicate estimate changes when scope increases during a BDC migration project?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Jab BDC migration project ke dauran scope increase hota hai, main immediately change document karta hoon: original scope assumption, kya change hua, revised effort breakdown, aur timeline impact. Main project manager ko written mein communicate karta hoon additional work absorb karne se pehle — change request ya scope change note. Message clearly state karta hai: original estimate specific assumptions pe based tha, woh assumptions change hue, yahan revised effort aur timeline impact hai, aur yahan change manage karne ke options hain (resources add karo, scope reduce karo, timeline extend karo). Silent scope absorption risk hai — yeh visibility ke bina overrun cause karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When scope increases during a BDC migration project, I immediately document the change: original scope assumption, what changed, revised effort breakdown, and timeline impact. I communicate this in writing to the project manager before absorbing additional work — a change request or scope change note. The message clearly states: original estimate was based on specific assumptions, those assumptions changed, here is the revised effort and timeline impact, and here are options to manage the change (add resources, reduce scope, extend timeline). Silent scope absorption is a risk — it causes overrun without visibility.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Midway through project: client added 3 new migration objects. Developer said 'I'll manage.' 2 weeks later: project was 10 days late, developer was stressed, client was upset. Correct action: immediately escalate to PM — 'Adding 3 objects adds 12 days. Options: extend by 12 days, add another developer, or defer 3 objects to Phase 2.' Let PM and client decide — not developer.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Agreeing to additional scope without formally documenting the estimate impact — being helpful in the moment causes project failure later. Always escalate scope changes formally.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Scope change impact template (email format)
" Subject: Scope Change Alert — BDC Migration Project
" Original scope: Customer master only (5000 records, 1 transaction)
" Original estimate: 8 project days
" Change requested: Add vendor master + open items (15000 records, 2 transactions)
" Additional effort breakdown:
"   Vendor master BDC: 4 days
"   Open items BAPI:   3 days
"   Additional testing: 3 days
"   Additional mock cutover: 1 day
"   Total addition: 11 days
" Revised estimate: 19 project days
" Timeline impact: +11 days unless additional resource assigned
" Decision required by: [date] to maintain go-live

followupAnswerBank.question: What buffers do you add to BDC estimation and why?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BDC estimation mein main apply karta hoon yeh buffers: standard scope uncertainty ke liye 20% (requirements jo clear lagti hain unmein aksar hidden edge cases hote hain), multiple clarification rounds need karne wale unclear requirements ke liye 30%, developer specific SAP module ke liye new hone par 40%, jab source data quality unknown ho aur discovery phase chahiye 25%, stakeholder review aur sign-off cycles ke liye 15%, multi-system landscapes mein transport ke baad regression testing ke liye 10%. Main maximum total buffer 50% apply karta hoon — uske beyond main fixed-price commitment ke jagah phased billing wala time-and-material engagement recommend karta hoon, kyunki >50% uncertainty wale fixed estimates project risks hain estimates nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Buffers I apply in BDC estimation: 20% for standard scope uncertainty (requirements that seem clear often have hidden edge cases), 30% for unclear requirements needing multiple clarification rounds, 40% when the developer is new to the specific SAP module, 25% when source data quality is unknown and a discovery phase is needed, 15% for stakeholder review and sign-off cycles, 10% for regression testing after transport in multi-system landscapes. The maximum total buffer I apply is 50% — beyond that I recommend a time-and-material engagement with phased billing rather than a fixed-price commitment, because fixed estimates with >50% uncertainty are project risks not estimates.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Fixed price project: estimated 15 days with 20% buffer = 18 days. Actual: 26 days. Lost 8 days of revenue. Root cause: source data quality was completely unknown — first time seeing legacy data revealed 30 distinct date formats, 500 obsolete code values, duplicate records. Should have recommended T&M with discovery phase or applied 40% uncertainty buffer for unknown data quality.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Applying a flat 10-20% buffer to all estimates regardless of uncertainty level — low-uncertainty projects don't need it, high-uncertainty projects need 30-50% or a T&M engagement recommendation.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Buffer calculation helper
DATA: lv_base_days    TYPE p DECIMALS 1 VALUE '8.0',
      lv_buffer_pct   TYPE p DECIMALS 2,
      lv_final_days   TYPE p DECIMALS 1.
" Requirements clarity: clear=1.20, partial=1.30, unclear=1.50
lv_buffer_pct = 1.20.  " Clear requirements
" Data quality unknown: add 25%
lv_buffer_pct = lv_buffer_pct + 0.25.
" New developer: add 40%
" lv_buffer_pct = lv_buffer_pct + 0.40.  " Uncomment if applicable
lv_final_days = lv_base_days * lv_buffer_pct.
WRITE: / |Base estimate : { lv_base_days } days|.
WRITE: / |Buffer factor: { lv_buffer_pct }|.
WRITE: / |Final estimate: { lv_final_days } days|.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort when the client says 'just a simple migration' but you suspect complexity?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Jab client migration ko 'simple' describe karta hai, main estimate confirm karne se pehle sample file request karta hoon. 20-50 record sample reveal karta hai: described se actual column count, date format variety, mapping tables require karne wale code values, data quality issues, aur unexpected mandatory fields. Main sample analyze karta hoon aur estimate ko base karta hoon kya data actually dikhata hai uspe, jo client ne describe kiya uspe nahi. Agar complexity find hoti hai, main data ko evidence ke roop mein use karke explain karta hoon — 'Aapke date column mein 5 different formats hain jo robust conversion routine require karte hain — yeh 1.5 days add karta hai.' Evidence-based estimates gut-feel revisions se better accept hote hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When a client describes a migration as 'simple,' I request a sample file before confirming the estimate. A 20-50 record sample reveals: actual column count versus described, date format variety, code values requiring mapping tables, data quality issues, and unexpected mandatory fields. I analyze the sample and base the estimate on what the data actually shows, not what the client described. If complexity is found, I explain it with the data as evidence — 'Your date column has 5 different formats which requires a robust conversion routine — that adds 1.5 days.' Evidence-based estimates are accepted better than gut-feel revisions.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Client: 'Simple vendor migration, maybe 2 days?' I asked for sample file. Sample: 60 columns (expected 20), 4 bank account types in legacy needing different BDC screens, date formats in German locale, vendors in multiple company codes. Revised estimate: 8 days. Client surprised but accepted when I showed sample file analysis. If I had said '2 days' without looking, we would have had a problem by day 3.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Accepting 'simple' at face value and estimating without data — the word 'simple' is the client's perception, not technical reality. Always verify with actual data before committing to an estimate.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Quick complexity assessment from sample file (30 min analysis)
DATA: lv_col_count   TYPE i,
      lt_date_fmts   TYPE string_table,
      lt_code_values TYPE string_table.
" Count columns in header row
SPLIT lt_raw[ 1 ]-data AT ',' INTO TABLE DATA(lt_cols).
lv_col_count = lines( lt_cols ).
WRITE: / |Columns found: { lv_col_count }| COLOR COL_TOTAL.
" Check date format variety
LOOP AT lt_raw FROM 2 ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<r>).
  SPLIT <r>-data AT ',' INTO TABLE lt_cols.
  IF lt_cols[ 5 ]-table_line CA '-'. INSERT 'YYYY-MM-DD' INTO TABLE lt_date_fmts. ENDIF.
  IF lt_cols[ 5 ]-table_line CA '/'. INSERT 'MM/DD/YYYY' INTO TABLE lt_date_fmts. ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.
SORT lt_date_fmts. DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM lt_date_fmts.
WRITE: / |Date format variants: { lines( lt_date_fmts ) }|.
